title: Could China and Russia really destroy Starlink? Only with a boomerang.
gist: A joint investigation by The Insider, Der Spiegel, and Le Monde found leaked documents showing China and Russia have run a structured, multi-year military-technical cooperation program specifically aimed at countering SpaceX's Starlink, with a Chinese-proposed 'escalation ladder' running from legal pressure to jamming to physical/cyber destruction, and experts telling Ars that some of those steps are already underway.

insights.claim: Leaked documents reviewed by The Insider, Der Spiegel, and Le Monde reveal a previously undisclosed China-Russia Military-Technical Cooperation Forum (first held in 2023, with a sixth session planned for late this year in St. Petersburg) that has produced a structured, multi-disciplinary program including efforts specifically targeting Starlink.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Establishes that anti-Starlink planning is an institutionalized, recurring bilateral program rather than ad hoc rhetoric, raising the credibility of downstream military/ASAT risk to SpaceX's LEO constellation and any commercial or government payloads riding on Starlink-derived buses.
insights.quote: The documents show a partnership that has moved well beyond shared rhetoric into a structured, multi-disciplinary program to build weapons neither country could develop alone

insights.claim: Chinese aerospace state enterprise CASC presented a three-step 'escalation ladder' for countering Starlink at the 2023 forum: first legal/diplomatic pressure and collision-risk framing, then regulatory spectrum/orbital-slot filings plus electromagnetic jamming, and finally physical destruction via cyberattack and anti-satellite weapons such as projectile clouds.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
entities: China Aerospace Science and Technology Corporation
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete staged threat model for Starlink/SpaceX and, by extension, any LEO mega-constellation operator (Kuiper, Guowang, NRO proliferated constellations) — jamming and regulatory obstruction are the near-term risks, kinetic/cyber destruction the tail risk.
insights.locator: So, how might China and Russia go about countering Starlink? section

insights.claim: Charles Galbreath of the Mitchell Institute for Aerospace Studies says the China-Russia anti-Starlink escalation ladder is not merely theoretical: 'I do think we are a couple rungs up that ladder,' with jamming and regulatory pressure already occurring.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: An expert assessment that active countermeasures are already underway (not just planned) should raise near-term risk premiums for satellite operators and defense contractors exposed to LEO communications infrastructure.
insights.quote: I do think we are a couple rungs up that ladder

insights.claim: China's recent recovery of its first reusable orbital-class rocket booster could allow Chinese companies to ramp launch cadence and rapidly build out mega-constellations like Guowang, closing the gap with Starlink's current fleet of more than 10,000 satellites.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Reusable-booster progress is the key leading indicator to watch for China closing SpaceX's launch-cadence moat; a faster Guowang buildout would reduce China's economic incentive to attack Starlink and instead compete directly.
insights.locator: paragraph on China's reusable booster recovery

insights.claim: Russia is already jamming Starlink receivers on the ground, China has reportedly developed a powerful ground-based microwave weapon capable of threatening LEO satellites, and NATO intelligence is monitoring a Russian concept to eject small pellet clouds into a constellation's orbit.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Multiple concrete, already-deployed or in-development counter-satellite capabilities (ground jamming, microwave weapons, kinetic pellet clouds) constitute real operational risk to any LEO communications or imaging asset, not just Starlink.

insights.claim: The US Space Force has unveiled its own ground-based satellite jammer, indicating the anti-satellite escalation is becoming symmetric among major powers rather than one-sided.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Confirms a broader trend of militarization of LEO among the US, China, and Russia, which should be priced into any long-horizon defense-space investment thesis (jamming/counter-jamming, resilient satcom demand).
insights.locator: paragraph on US Space Force jammer

insights.claim: A Russian nuclear detonation in low-Earth orbit — a scenario Russia has reportedly explored and which would violate the 1967 Outer Space Treaty — could be the 'ultimate Starlink killer' but would also render much of LEO unusable for months or years, crippling Russia's and China's own satellites too, making it a mutually destructive last resort.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: The mutual-destruction dynamic of a nuclear LEO strike makes it the least likely near-term scenario despite being the most catastrophic, meaning investors should weight jamming/regulatory/cyber risk far more heavily than the more dramatic kinetic/nuclear tail risk.
insights.locator: paragraph on Russia's reported orbital nuclear weapon plan

insights.claim: Mitchell Institute's Galbreath warns that any successful Chinese/Russian countermeasure against Starlink would put at risk the full range of LEO-dependent missions, including commercial imaging constellations like Planet's Dove fleet, communications, imagery, and the Pentagon's proposed Golden Dome missile shield.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Names specific non-SpaceX commercial exposure (Planet Labs' Dove constellation) and a major US defense program (Golden Dome) as downstream casualties of successful anti-Starlink countermeasures — a concrete watchlist for LEO-dependent equities and contractors.
insights.quote: Any mission set that you can execute from low-Earth orbit becomes at risk by the mechanisms or means that China and Russia could develop to counter SpaceX and Starlink

insights.claim: Starlink has been a decisive military enabler for Ukraine since Russia's 2022 invasion, extending the effective reach of Ukrainian weapons deep into Russian territory, which is a primary driver of Russian and Chinese motivation to develop countermeasures.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Frames the underlying strategic rationale: proven battlefield efficacy of commercial satcom is what is drawing state-level attack investment, a pattern likely to repeat for any future commercial dual-use space infrastructure.
insights.locator: paragraph on Starlink's role in Ukraine

top_entities: China Aerospace Science and Technology Corporation
